title: App Router Introduction
content:
### App Router Overview Introduced in Next.js 13, the App Router is a new paradigm built on **React Server Components** and streaming. It lives in the `app/` directory alongside (or replacing) the traditional `pages/` directory. ### Special Files The `app/` directory uses a convention of special files: | File | Purpose | |------|---------| | `page.js` | UI for a route | | `layout.js` | Shared wrapper for a segment | | `loading.js` | Loading UI (Suspense boundary) | | `error.js` | Error boundary UI | | `not-found.js` | 404 UI | | `route.js` | API endpoint | | `template.js` | Re-rendered layout wrapper | ### Basic Structure ``` app/ layout.js # Root layout (required) page.js # Home page (/) about/ page.js # /about blog/ layout.js # Blog section layout page.js # /blog [slug]/ page.js # /blog/my-post ``` ### App vs Pages Router | Feature | Pages Router | App Router | |---------|-------------|------------| | Rendering | SSG, SSR | Server Components by default | | Data Fetching | getStaticProps, getServerSideProps | Direct async components, fetch() | | Layouts | Custom patterns | Built-in layout.js | | Streaming | Not supported | Supported via loading.js | | Mutations | Custom API routes | Server Actions | ### Getting Started To use the App Router, create an `app/` directory at your project root. Next.js will automatically prefer it over `pages/` for routes that exist in both. ```jsx // app/page.js — Home page export default function Home() { return

Hello, App Router!

; } // app/layout.js — Root layout (required) export default function RootLayout({ children }) { return ( {children} ); } ```
